# Test bash coverage collection via BASH_XTRACEFD - simplified version
env MCP_BASH_COVERAGE=1
env MCP_BASH_COVERAGE_DIR=$WORK/bash-coverage

# Check environment variables
echo 'MCP_BASH_COVERAGE='$MCP_BASH_COVERAGE
stdout 'MCP_BASH_COVERAGE=1'
echo 'MCP_BASH_COVERAGE_DIR='$MCP_BASH_COVERAGE_DIR
stdout 'MCP_BASH_COVERAGE_DIR='$WORK/bash-coverage

# Create a simple test script
exec bash -c 'echo "#!/bin/bash" > test_script.sh && echo "echo hello" >> test_script.sh && chmod 0755 test_script.sh'

# Run bash command with coverage enabled
bash './test_script.sh'
stdout 'hello'

# Create the coverage directory manually if needed
exec mkdir -p $WORK/bash-coverage

# Check if any trace files were created
exec bash -c 'ls $WORK/bash-coverage/ | grep -E "bash-.*\.trace" || echo "No trace files found"'

# Try a direct bash command
bash 'echo "Direct command"'
stdout 'Direct command'

# Let's examine what's happening with the trace file
exec bash -c 'cd $WORK/bash-coverage && ls -la'

# Let's look at the content of a trace file
exec bash -c 'cat $WORK/bash-coverage/bash-*.trace | head -10'
